speaker: interviewer
text: Tape four, tape four with Michael Kishel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What were the relationships between the people in Majdanek that you shared bunks with? Were they all from Warsaw, or were they from all over?

speaker: interviewee
text: We all came from Warsaw because most, most of the people that I came with were-- of course, they were from, from Warsaw but they were from out of Warsaw because they were sent in before that, sent in from other towns to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. But they were all Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all Polish Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did people--

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me--

speaker: interviewer
text: --treat each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. Excuse me. They came transports from other places also. I remember it came a transport from Greece. And they were very down. They couldn't speak any the language. Only they speak French, they could speak Greek and some of them couldn't even speak Hebrew. But no Jewish, no Polish, no German and they were suffering terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. But how did people get along?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they were-- could say we were friends, because we got friendly. And I remember that whatever-- wherever camp I was, I always found myself a bosom friend that I cared for him, and he cared for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this is how we survived. As a matter of fact, I'm still looking for one friend that wherever we were, they always thought there was this, we are two brothers. I had one friend, his name is Abram Wrobel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Abram--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wrobel. W-R-O-- with a point-- B-E-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wrobel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wrobel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Avram, where was he from?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came together to Majdanek.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't come to Majdanek. We were from Majdanek to Birkenau, we went together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were close, and everybody thought that we are brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he's alive and I met him on the street, on Delancey Street, on a car when he was riding to, to work and I was riding to work. And I recognized him from the back. I blew the horn, and I said, remember me, of course? And we've seen each other a few times. Occasionally we see each other. And there's another friend that I-- after being at another camp, that was after we were out of the camps on the-- on the march. I found another friend that we kept together, and that one was even more. Because I practically helped him out from the dead to alive. I used to have two of them, him and a friend of his from his town. I used to carry both of them, on my both sides, had to practically carry them and march so that they can survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the opposite side of that picture, people who weren't nice to each other? Were there such incidents--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, there were such-- there was such-- like, for instance, there were kapos, they were mean. There could be good kapos, and there could be mean kapos. As a matter of fact, when I came to Birkenau there was a kapo. He was from Krakow, a big tall man. And he was a good guy. He had to do-- if he wouldn't be kapo, there was another kapo. But he had a kapo with a heart. He came over and gave you a push and if you thought that was bad, that was wrong. He gave you a push because he didn't want you-- someone else to give you the push that you should drop dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one time we had a, a gathering of, of America Holocaust survivors at the hotel, the Brown Hotel in the Catskills. And I was there doing something for the organization. And I was walking, and who comes across to me? That kapo with a wife and a daughter. And I said I don't know if you remember me, but I remember you. You were a kapo, but you were a good kapo. His wife and his daughter's eyes show-- showed. They were so happy. Yeah. Have you ever run into other kapos?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I looked for one that was a bad one. But I heard the people that used to be with him, and he didn't show up. He must have been-- must have died or killed him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did people ever hurt kapos? Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --ever gang up against them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but during the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: You couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: You couldn't. Were they armed?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was law. It was anything--

speaker: interviewer
text: But were they armed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they were not armed. They could-- we could not get any arm--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but the kapos?

speaker: interviewee
text: No kapos were-- it was armed with a club.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: But no arms like guns or anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they take advantage of some of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: At various times, they used to tell you to work more or to-- I did not have a chance to give a kapo a chance to take a wrench to me. I always try to stay away from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. In what way did some people get special treatment?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- and some people, from a kapo, I remember, there was in Birkenau I believe, there was a man that had pieces of gold from where they had it. But they wanted to, to be good, to be secure that he'll have a piece of bread and this and that. He gave a kapo some money, some of it, that he should give him an-- excuse me-- an extra piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he gave it to him. But this kapo or the man, they were here today and they were going to be tomorrow someplace else, because it was not up to the kapo to keep him. They used to send out-- away transports to other places. Mhm. So special treatment was not always--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was not always working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Were you ever punished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got lashes. There was in 1943, towards the end of the year, sometimes in November or December, there was a transport. Before I'm going to tell you the story, is when I was in Majdanek I was talking with my brother, when there comes a transport, we should push ourselves to go on a transport. To say-- the slogan was, the bowl of water and soup and the piece of bread I'll get someplace else also.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we pushed ourself to a transport. He got on a transport first and went to a town called Skarzysko. They were working for military supplies and different [INAUDIBLE]. And I couldn't make that transport. And I was-- then it came next time, I pushed myself and I came into Birkenau, to Auschwitz. That was Auschwitz to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came there, we got a tattoo, 129354.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, can we show it on the tape, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. This is with a triangle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what does the trianle--

speaker: interviewee
text: The triangle means that I'm Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that time, we found out that they are different. There's some red, red, and the other, they had green triangles. They were homosexuals. Red ones were political prisoners. There were different-- transfer different people. We had a transfer-- this kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: And everybody in that transferred was-- or did they mix them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody that came to Birkenau, everybody that came to Auschwitz got numbered, tattooed.

speaker: interviewer
text: But when you went on a transport to a different camp, to a labor camp, did they put political and homosexual--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Together?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were together but only the sign, because you had a number here. You also had a number on the jacket.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the pants.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: The number. And that green or red insignia was shown so the kapo or the German SS men should know that this man is because he's a homosexual, or this man is because he is against the, the political views of the German Reich as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your brother went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother went to a labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Labor camp, and you went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to Auschwitz-Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was in Birkenau for a few months, we were working, and there were a few-- a few camps. There was the men's camp. There was the women's camp. There was a hospital camp. There was a gypsy camp. On one side with us was the gypsies, and on the other side were the women, the women's camp. But the women's camp was a little further out. We could shout to them, but we couldn't see them. But the gypsy camp was next to it. Beyond the gypsy camp was the hospital camp. But people used to be sick or they used to take the-- who wanted to heal them, whether they were good, just maybe minor things. And we-- they took us to work in the hospital camp to straighten the streets out, the road, because there was a lot of clay. And as soon as you went in to step into clay, and you tried to get out, you went in with the other foot, and it would straighten out. Next to the hospital camp was the crematoria. So we've seen what was going on, all this time. And it came to us to see, well, if a transport comes, I'm going to push myself. He says, only far away from the crematory. But we didn't realize that when they want you, they're just going to bring you back. And a matter of fact, they did, because when we went, Landsman from my own town, which I met him in, in, in-- he came over from Majdanek, but I didn't seem him in Majdanek. He came with me together to Birkenau, . Went to that camp, to a new camp called Lagischa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a camp to work. Was a concentration camp under guard. And we were working there making-- there was a factory making synthetic rubber, synthetic fuel, and all kinds of other manufacturing, which we did-- which we did work there. And being there-- being there was much, I would say the beginning was much easier because we didn't have that much supervision. But we couldn't run away. But when it came to wintertime, it was the end of November, beginning December, when it came to cold weather, there was very little supplies, no heat. To wash yourself, you had to go outside. There was water running, sometimes it was frozen. We had to wash ourself with the snow. And if there was-- it was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we see that we did not make such a good deal running away. Then our health was going down, and down, and down. Of course, we got beaten on the-- from very little food. I got beaten on the-- on the workplace, where I worked. And one time I got beaten up, that my scar-- my-- Your skull.

speaker: interviewee
text: My skull, my skull was beaten up here in such a way that was blood was running all the time because the blood wasn't red. The blood was rose, a rose color. It was so thin, that it could never heal.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe the blood has a little fat that helps it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And why were you beaten? Why were you punished?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because that kapo or the German didn't do right or he saw-- or sometimes we were beaten-- one, one haftling, one prisoner, beat with another. Why? Because the SS men want to have some fun.

speaker: interviewer
text: They said, go over. He did-- does not work with you as you do. Hit him because he's not working good. So they want me to hit him. How can I hit him? So I went over and I make myself hit him. They say, what? Did you hit him? That's not good. He tells him to hit me. This is how they made the two of us fight each other. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyhow, I got for as-- maybe for something like that. I don't remember exactly, and I got beaten up. It came down from there to take transports again. Before, they used to take a-- they took a transport or two and sent to the crematoria, back to Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they came and they took people that they needed people in-- in another camp called [INAUDIBLE]. [INAUDIBLE] was a camp-- a camp that they worked in coal mines.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: That people come out, stronger people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And being that I was not strong enough, the guy didn't-- the SS man didn't want to take me. So he put me on the side. Those that I wanted to remain standing. Those I didn't want outside. I wanted to leave, so I sneaked in to that place again. Maybe I make myself strong from like this with my bust out, like a big, strong man. One blow, I would stay on the floor, lay on the ground. And he recognized me because he says, hey, you. I told you to go down to there. And he says, before I finish, you should-- you should see me. And the blockalteste-- the blockalteste was the person that-- who is responsible for the people, for the-- for us, in the barrack. He was-- he was a Jew, one of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- not to say a friend of mine, but he was-- I was good with him, he was good with me, because-- and he came over to the SS man. Hey, to me-- give him-- hit me in the face and don't you-- I'll see you later. You're going to get this, and this, and this. Let it be-- let it come to that, I'll be happy. Because he'll give me that I'll-- with hard, and feeling. But anyhow, this helped me get, get the hands of that SS man off me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I did get a few lashes, and I got some other lashes on other places where we worked, because the work wasn't satisfactory, not fast enough. You know, they, they-- many reasons.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Quite a few times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go from Lagisza?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Lagischa, I was so down that it came in another transport. The other transport took us-- we didn't know where it was taking us, but at least-- whatever's going to be was going to be. It took us to another-- to another camp called Buna, Monowitz. And that was a paradise for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was it a paradise?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because there was-- the first week or 10 days, we were walked around in the camp, we didn't do a thing. We just ate and stayed--

speaker: interviewer
text: They gave you food? Good food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us food, and we stayed-- most of the time, we stayed with the kitchen, finished the cooking to, to bring back the, the, the, the barrels of food. Also we used to lick him out to get more food. Because when they gave out, they had to be cleaned. We used to clean them, and this was food galore. And then we had to go to work. And we went to work in-- In Buna, we went to work to the-- for the Carbide Werke, carbide producing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Ammunition.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, carbide is not ammunition. Carbide is-- no is this coal, like white coal. You put it in water, and it gives out gas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what I mean?

speaker: interviewer
text: Not quite.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a kind of-- it's a kind of piece-- it's in pieces like coal. And, and this, when it gets into-- with water, it disintegrates and gives out fumes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And what is it used for?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's used for gas, for fuel.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. I see. So that was in Buna.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Buna.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of these, were they part of the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Auschwitz camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Auschwitz-Birkenau complex.

speaker: interviewee
text: Auschwitz is the main-- the main place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, and these were satellite camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Auschwitz is the main-- the main-- it's the central office. And Auschwitz has itself, itself also a lot of crematoria. But the main crematoria was in Birkenau.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And all the other camps were built additional, after Auschwitz brought them in. Says, all right. We need to make a-- because the Germans that produced material for the war needed workers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they could not have 10,000 workers in one place, because they couldn't handle so many. They coming in and going out so much, you lose all the time. So they made smaller camps around the area. In this town or this area, we'll make this kind of, of material. The other one will make a different one. And this is how they produced so much--

speaker: interviewer
text: The war machine.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Raw material.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And how long were you in Buna?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Buna until January 18, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we went on the march because the, the war was getting closer to the camps, and the Russians were getting closer. And the Germans were afraid to, to stay there. And although they still, still defend-- defended the areas beyond the camps, beyond Buna, but they start to evacuate us and on January 18, all the Auschwitz camps and the affiliates started to go out on a march. And we walked from Buna to Gleiwitz. Gleiwitz was a larger town. And there they loaded us up on a railroad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. How much did you know about the war coming to an end?

speaker: interviewee
text: At a later date--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Because first of all, we already knew that if they taking the camps-- the camps out, that means the war was getting closer. And they're losing, they're going back. And we saw a lot of planes riding around because there was a time that we haven't seen a Russian plane or American plane or plane any-- flying around our area. But later on, we did see planes and one time there was a bombing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Buna. That we knew they was not German planes. They was other ones. So then came the time that they evacuated the Auschwitz complex for the inland. When we came to Gleiwitz, they could not go. They supplied us with the railroad to go on a-- on a railroad. But they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Why couldn't they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because it was too many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they need-- they needed more guards. This way, they got it in their-- on a train. They locked the doors. And then they are safe, and we cannot run out from the inside. But we didn't go much, because the rails were closed, were bombed. And we were going back and forth and back and forth, and we didn't know why. Until we came one-- about two days going back and forth, we see we're still in the same place. They took us out and were marched inside Germany. And there was [GERMAN], it was like Lower Silesia. In that part we walked from one place to another, and we didn't have places where to sleep. So we came, one time we came to a town called Waldenburg. Waldenburg's spelled W-A-L-D-E-N-B-U-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Waldenburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Waldenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: W-A-L-D-E-N-B-U-R-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Waldenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Waldenbork or Waldenburg?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, it doesn't really matter. I'll find it.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was Waldenburg. Walden is, is forest, burg is mountains.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did, did you pass German houses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anybody say anything, talk?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anybody come and look?

speaker: interviewee
text: We could not talk to them. Nobody came out and gave us water. When we came to Waldenburg, it was evening. And we had to go-- they did not have a place where to put us in. There was a mine in Waldenburg. What kind of mine it was, I don't know, but they pushed us into the mine and closed the door. We were there, a few thousand of us. And from the heat, and the smell, and the-- and the-- whatever it was, many of us died in that-- in that thing. When we came out there, I took my two friends that we got together, one as well was-- looked like me, like my brother, also blonde, and a friend of his-- excuse me for the expression, they peed in their pants. They made in their-- terrible. Coming down from-- there was a little lake. I washed them up, I cleaned them up. And there were all the civilians, civilian workers and prisoners of war, French prisoners of war were there also. And I don't know things, because the French I could recognize their language they were talking. And civilian workers, Poles and French. And they [INAUDIBLE] civilian workers, also on a march to go away, but not under the German supervision.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went out to beg from the civilian workers to give us some bread. Who supervised them? Under what--

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't need any supervision because they were free. They were not under the--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were being watched by whom?

speaker: interviewee
text: By the Latvians, and by the Ukrainians, by the Lithuanians and by the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there also Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were under the German rule. They were under, under the German contract. I mean, they were Volksdeutsche maybe or--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to the civilians, the French or the other ones, and begged them for bread or other things. And I brought it to my friends and myself. And we got something to eat. And it was the best meal we ever had. And then we started to go on a march again. And we came to a place somewhere in, in other area, maybe 30, 40, or 50 miles away. And they took us to work. We lived in a village, and each village had stalls where they'd keep their, their products, or hay or, or straw, whatever it is. And there we slept. Each one of us came at night. They closed us in, and the guards were outside. In the morning, we went out to work and dig ditches on mountains. And we could see from the farthest, there's some movement. The Russians were on the other side. We were working on the front, digging ditches for the defense of the Germans. And this is how to was until May 10, the last day of May. We heard that Berlin gave up because the Germans themselves, they already knew that they hooked. They don't know what to do with. It came the last day, all the group, maybe five, six Germans got up together. And they had a conversation, what to do with us. We were maybe 200, 300 of us. We left, thousands. We remained about 200, 300 of us. What to do with us. They finally stood there and they start to move away. You know, left us standing. By themselves. It was already evening, and we heard the artillery knocking here and there. And we couldn't sleep. We were in stalls, each one separately. We separated not to be all of us at the same time. And we were hiding. It came in the morning, no Germans. And we started to go out on the street. We saw the Russians. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It was a new life. We came to-- we were hungry. We, we had a pail. We went into German houses. I had the uniform, the striped uniform I want to get rid of. I went into a German house because the Germans run away because the Russians came forward. They were afraid. And I got a jacket from a German organization. It's called TOD. T-O-D. The TOD Organization, they were supplying labor. They were a labor organization supplying the Germans with technical things. And they had a special uniform. As a matter of fact, the picture that I made, my first picture on the liberation, I have-- had that in the-- in that jacket. And I went into the house. I got myself a towel, one or two shirts, some underwear, whatever they had leftover because whether it fit or didn't fit, it [INAUDIBLE] fit me right. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we started to go-- to go home, to go to Poland, to Warsaw. And there, there was a train. We asked where the train was going. The train was going towards Warsaw. The train was going to Warsaw, and we went in-- went into the train, and they took us up to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, but who? Who? The Russians?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it was already-- the Poles were already-- the Russians and the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh-huh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came with the train towards Warsaw. Warsaw was bombed. You couldn't, couldn't go-- [INAUDIBLE] was one, one-- the west side of Warsaw. I knew Warsaw well, like I know New York because from before the war. So I started walking from one end of Warsaw to the other end of Warsaw to cross the Vistula in a-- in a boat, and came to a train station, which takes me east, because I was east of Warsaw. I came to there to get a train, and the train is leaving for-- from another place. The other place is about 10, 15 kilometers away. Well, I walked so much, I'll walk 15 kilometers more. I came there. The only place that I could reserve was on the roof.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why were you going east? Why did you want to go east?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because my, my-- I was east of Warsaw, my [NON-ENGLISH] My town was east of Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wanted to go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I want to go home. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I, I, I drag myself up on the roof, and I had a little suitcase, because I took the towels, a shirt, a pair of pants. And I had it. And there was a conductor, a woman, a Russian woman. She was conducting. And she says-- she saw me. Well, this is all right. When came to the station, I had to, to get off. The train didn't stop. So I says, I'll jump. She says, go ahead, jump. I'll throw the suitcase in. I said, I'm not that dumb. I threw the suitcase first. [SOBBING] And I jumped out. Nothing happened to me. I had had experience. And I walked 5 kilometers to town. And I see, as he approaches me, a young men that I know. When he noticed me, he was afraid, because I looked like a dead man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know who I am. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And he said, my brother's home. He told me that my brother came home. [CRYING] That's all I needed. So we were there for a month or two. We went to Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your house still--

speaker: interviewee
text: My house was still there, and there lived-- Poles lived there. They took me in. I was there almost for two weeks, sleeping and eating.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Poles were people you knew?

speaker: interviewee
text: People I knew. They were neighbors of ours.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were they nice to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they were very nice to me. A matter of fact, one was a friend of my brother. And he came over, he wanted to come to the States. Signed for him that he'll be responsible for him for the time being. He came here and remained here as an immigrant.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: We sold him the house. We sold him the house that we had. And after a few weeks, we went away to, to Warsaw and to Lodz, too, because there-- a few weeks before my brother came--

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few weeks before my brother came, there was an incident that a man that lived in Kaluszyn, he was a kind of communist, left-- socialist, left winger-- and he wanted to become Mr. Big Shot. And because of that, they got away with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the few Jews that had the factory of the-- of the sheepskin, they were hidden by, by farmers. They came back. They had to leave because they were afraid they were going to kill them. So you left--

speaker: interviewee
text: So when my brother and I came back, the Poles, the [NON-ENGLISH], the, the, the, the army, they call it the false army. They came to us and says, you don't have to be afraid. We will do nothing to you. You are-- like, we were saints to them. They didn't-- they didn't do anything but we didn't want to stay there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to Warsaw and to Lodz and from there.
